title: SkarionBooks Lab — QuickBooks Simulator
subhead: SkarionBooks is our custom QuickBooks simulator — a full SPA with a chart of accounts, journal entry posting, AR invoicing, cash receipts, bank reconciliation, and financial reports. No paid QBO trial required.
hook: "Do you actually know your way around a GL system, or only the textbook?" SkarionBooks lets you prove it: open the simulator, post the entries you learned in Modules 02–06, and produce real reports off real Skarion Engineering numbers.

chunks.title: Scenario judgment check — before SkarionBooks
chunks.intro: Before we go hands-on in SkarionBooks, let's make sure your judgment on real workplace situations is locked in. Type your answer for each — there's no multiple choice, because the work is in the reasoning.

questions.q: You are posting the AP batch and notice invoice #0891 is the same vendor, same amount, same date as invoice #0847 you posted two weeks ago. What do you do?
questions.explanation: Likely duplicate. Hold #0891, do not pay. Compare line-by-line to #0847, confirm with the vendor in writing. If a true duplicate, void it and document. Never pay a suspected duplicate on faith.

questions.q: A client sends a wire for $45,000 but your open invoice is for $47,500. The client didn't send remittance advice. What steps do you take before posting anything?
questions.explanation: Do not guess which invoice or post blind. Email/call the client for remittance advice, confirm whether $45,000 is a partial payment, a discounted payment, or a different invoice entirely. Once confirmed in writing, post the cash to the correct invoice and document the $2,500 short-pay with a dispute flag if needed.

questions.q: Your manager tells you to just "plug the difference" on the bank reconciliation because it's only $340 and the audit is tomorrow. What do you do?
questions.explanation: Push back professionally. Ask to walk through the business reason and note that plugging hides the real error and creates audit risk. Offer to trace the $340 to its source in the next hour. Never post a plug to force a reconciliation — escalate in writing if pressured.

questions.q: You notice that last month's accrual for offshore labor ($14,800) was never reversed on the first of this month. How does this affect the current month's financials and what do you do?
questions.explanation: The accrual is still sitting on the books, so COGS is overstated this month and Accrued Liabilities are overstated — IF the real invoice was also entered this month (double-counted). Reverse the missed accrual now (Dr Accrued Liabilities $14,800 / Cr COGS $14,800), document the correction, and confirm the real invoice booked once (net effect = one correct expense).

questions.q: A vendor is threatening to stop services unless you pay their invoice immediately. The invoice failed the 3-way match two weeks ago. What do you do?
questions.explanation: Do not pay a failed match under pressure. Acknowledge the urgency, escalate to the PM and controller, request the vendor send a corrected invoice or credit memo, and document the dispute. If services truly can't pause, get supervisor approval to pay the undisputed portion only — never the disputed amount.

chunks.title: What SkarionBooks is — and what it isn't
chunks.bodyHtml:
SkarionBooks is a self-contained ledger simulator built for this course. It comes pre-loaded with Skarion Engineering's full chart of accounts and opening balances, so you can post journal entries, create AR invoices, apply cash receipts, run a bank reconciliation, and generate a P&L and Balance Sheet — without needing a $600/year QuickBooks Online subscription.

Pre-loaded trial balance (opening): 47 accounts, ~$1.2M in opening Accounts Receivable, $340,000 in Operating Checking, $280,000 in Accounts Payable, a $500,000 term loan at 6.5%, and ~$2.1M in equity. Total debits equal total credits by construction — your job is to keep them that way as you post.

You do not configure the COA from scratch here; you did that work in Module 02. Here you use it: process invoices, post entries, reconcile, and report.

callout.html: Open SkarionBooks using the link in the lab below (it runs as a single-page app inside the course package). Keep it open in a second tab while you work through each task — post each entry in SkarionBooks, then come back here to check off the next step.

chunks.title: Chart of accounts review & first transactions
chunks.bodyHtml:
Lab task 1 — Review the COA. Open SkarionBooks and confirm every account is in the correct category: 1000s Assets, 2000s Liabilities, 3000s Equity, 4000s Revenue, 5000s COGS, 6000s Expenses. Flag and note any account that looks mis-categorized (there shouldn't be any — but verifying is the job).

Lab task 2 — Opening trial balance check. Total debits must equal total credits. Run the trial balance report and confirm it balances to the cent before you post a single new entry. This is the mechanical floor every close starts from.

Lab task 3 — Process three recent vendor invoices:

VendorInvoiceAmountCoding
Global CAD SolutionsGCS-2026-091$9,625.00Dr 5100 COGS — Subcontractor Drafting / Cr 2000 AP
ArcGIS Pro (annual renewal)ESRI-2026-RN$36,000.00Dr 1400 Prepaid Software / Cr 1000 Operating Checking
Office landlord (monthly rent)LEASE-2026-01$12,800.00Dr 6500 Software & Subscriptions → reclassify to Rent* / Cr 1000 Operating Checking

*If a dedicated Rent account exists in SkarionBooks, post monthly rent there; otherwise use the operating expense account indicated and note the classification. The point is to tie the coded account to the economic reality of the spend.

Lab task 4 — January payroll run. Post the full US payroll entry from Module 03: Dr 6000 Salaries — US Engineering PMs $79,167, Dr 6100 Payroll Tax Expense $6,256, Cr 2200 Payroll Tax Liabilities $27,312, Cr 1000 Operating Checking $58,111. Confirm debits equal credits ($85,423) and the trial balance still balances after posting.

chunks.title: Open SkarionBooks and complete the setup lab
chunks.instructionsHtml: Open SkarionBooks and complete all four setup-lab tasks above: COA review, opening trial-balance check, processing the three vendor invoices, and posting the January payroll run. After each entry, run the trial balance and confirm it still balances. Capture a screenshot of your completed trial balance.
chunks.reflectionPrompt: After posting all four tasks, what was your total Operating Checking balance, and did the trial balance still balance to the cent? Note any accounts you questioned during the COA review.

chunks.title: AR invoice, cash receipt, and bank reconciliation
chunks.bodyHtml:
Now run the full revenue and cash cycle in SkarionBooks:

  1. Create the AR invoice for the Tennessee Corridor HLD milestone: invoice Atlantic Fiber Co. for $480,000 (40% of the $1.2M contract). Coding: Dr 1100 Accounts Receivable $480,000 / Cr 4000 Engineering Design Revenue $480,000.
  2. Record the incoming wire from Atlantic Fiber Co. — $480,000 gross, less the $25 incoming wire fee, netting $479,975 to Operating Checking. Coding: Dr 1000 $479,975, Dr 6900 Bank Fees Expense $25, Cr 1100 AR $480,000.
  3. Apply the payment to the open invoice in the AR subledger and confirm the invoice is cleared and no longer aging.
  4. Post the bank fee correcting entry and confirm the fee is isolated in 6900.
  5. Run the bank reconciliation for January — match every transaction to the provided bank statement until the adjusted balances both equal $336,250.00, including the DEP-0091 duplicate deposit correction (Dr 1100 AR $10,547.10 / Cr 1000 Cash $10,547.10).
  6. Generate January P&L and Balance Sheet — screenshot both. The P&L should show $480,000 revenue, ~$140,000 COGS, and converge on ~$242,000 net income (per Module 06).

chunks.title: Complete the AR, cash, and bank rec lab in SkarionBooks
chunks.instructionsHtml: Work through all six tasks in the chunk above. The key proof points: the Atlantic Fiber invoice clears to zero after the wire is applied, the bank reconciliation converges on $336,250.00, and the January P&L lands near $242,000 net income. Capture screenshots of (1) the cleared AR subledger, (2) the completed bank reconciliation, and (3) the P&L.
chunks.reflectionPrompt: Did your bank reconciliation converge to exactly $336,250.00? If not, what was the remaining unexplained item, and how did you finally trace it? Paste your final P&L net income figure.
